questions.directive: Read a text-message chain.
questions.prompt: t-message chain. What can be concluded about Eli?

passage.text:
Hey Eli. I'm having a problem with my mini fridge. I hear the motor
humming, but it's not cooling off. David from our history class tells me
you're good at fixing things like this.
Could you come by and have a look?
Hey Elena. I'd be happy to take a look. Right now I'm in the library with
my math study group. We just got here.
Oh, I didn't mean right away. Any time is good.
How about tomorrow at 12:30?
My economics class starts at noon. Does 1:30 work?
That works fine. Chances are it's a broken condenser coil. I've got an
extra one, so I'll bring it with me.
Thank you so much!

options.text: He works at an appliance repair shop.
He has repaired refrigerators in the past.
He is studying to be a mechanical engineer.
He meets with his math study group every week.

questions.directive: Read an article.
questions.prompt: What is the main topic of the article? A community's resilience during a disruption

passage.text:
Blackout Brings Creativity to Finals Week

A sudden power outage during finals week left Smithfield students scrambling for solutions. Students took advantage of their smart-phone flashlights, and university staff provided camping lights for dorm lounges. "It was stressful but kind of fun," said sophomore Grace Lee. The blackout lasted six hours, caused by a transformer failure near campus. Dining services offered free coffee, and professors extended deadlines to ease the pressure. "We made the best of it," said senior Omar Davis, who studied with his bike light. Social media buzzed with photos of candlelit study sessions, turning the inconvenience into a moment of campus unity. Some students even organized impromptu acoustic concerts on the quad, proving that creativity thrives under constraints. "It reminded us that community matters more than convenience," said Professor Laura Chen. By the early morning hours, power was restored, but the evening's events left a lasting impression.

options.text: A new energy-saving initiative
A musical event on campus
An announcement about a technology upgrade

questions.directive: Read the passage and choose the best answer.
questions.prompt: The main purpose of this passage is to

passage.text: Veganism, a dietary approach that excludes all animal-based foods, has experienced notable shifts in popularity in the United States. A study conducted in 2020 showed a dramatic increase in the number of Americans adopting a plant-based diet between 2004 and 2019. This growth was fueled by expanding awareness of animal welfare, environmental concerns linked to industrial agriculture, and increased emphasis on healthy eating. During the 2010s, companies began to market plant- based meat alternatives that became highly popular with both restaurants and home cooks. By the mid-2020s, however, momentum began to level off. Surveys in 2025 indicated that only about three to four percent of Americans identified as vegan. Several factors contributed to this plateau. Preparing vegan meals can be more involved than preparing meat-based meals. For example, a vegan cheese substitute can be made with nuts, but the process is time- consuming. And while home cooks can buy commercially manufactured vegan foods, those foods are highly processed, raising concerns about their healthfulness. Additionally, some social media influencers have begun to promote increased protein consumption, sparking increased consumer demand for meat. American food trends come and go, but while some consumers adopt extreme diets, Americans overall seem to be favoring more flexibility in their eating habits.

options.text: describe the birth of veganism in the United States
list the benefits of a vegan diet
analyze some changes in Americans' attitudes toward veganism
identify trends in American consumer preferences

questions.directive: Read the passage and choose the best answer.
questions.prompt: According to the passage, what significant achievement in fusion research occurred in 1957?

passage.text:
Magnetic confinement fusion is a promising approach for generating clean electricity. This technology fuses deuterium and tritium, heavy forms of hydrogen, to release energy while producing minimal radioactive waste when compared to current technologies to produce nuclear power. The basic principle was first demonstrated in 1957: powerful magnetic fields contain extremely hot plasma at temperatures reaching 150 million degrees Celsius-ten times hotter than the sun's core. Without magnetic containment, the plasma would instantly cool upon touching reactor walls, halting the fusion process. Since those early breakthroughs, researchers have built increasingly sophisticated magnetic confinement reactors called tokamaks. The International Thermonuclear Experimental Reactor (ITER), currently under construction, represents the culmination of decades of magnetic confinement research. ITER aims to demonstrate sustained fusion reactions that produce more energy than they consume-a critical milestone called net

However, significant challenges remain before magnetic fusion can generate commercial electricity. Current experiments require enormous energy inputs to maintain plasma conditions, and the intense neutron radiation gradually damages reactor materials. Additionally, converting fusion energy into usable electricity requires developing efficient heat-to-power conversion systems. Despite these obstacles, successful demonstration of net energy gain could pave the way for fusion power plants by

options.text: Researchers successfully fused deuterium and tritium atoms for the first time.
Researchers demonstrated that magnetic fields could contain extremely hot plasma.
Researchers estimated the temperature of the sun's core to be 150 million degrees Celsius.
Researchers discovered the basic principle that explains the existence of heavy forms of hydrogen.

questions.directive: Read the passage and choose the best answer.

passage.text: generating clean electricity. This technology fuses deuterium and tritium, heavy forms of hydrogen, to release energy while producing minimal radioactive waste when compared to current technologies to produce nuclear power. Without magnetic containment, the plasma would instantly cool upon touching reactor walls, halting the fusion process. Since those early breakthroughs, researchers have built increasingly sophisticated magnetic confinement reactors called tokamaks. The International Thermonuclear Experimental Reactor (ITER), currently under construction, represents the culmination of decades of magnetic confinement research. ITER aims to demonstrate sustained fusion reactions that produce more energy than they consume -a critical milestone called net energy gain. However, significant challenges remain before magnetic fusion can generate commercial electricity. Current experiments require enormous energy inputs to maintain plasma conditions, and the intense neutron radiation gradually damages reactor materials. Additionally, converting fusion energy into usable electricity requires developing efficient heat-to-power conversion systems. Despite these obstacles, successful demonstration of net energy gain could pave the way for fusion power plants by the 2040s.

options.text: To explain how the plasma is contained within the reactor
To point out how researchers cool plasma after heating it for experiments
To demonstrate the final step in achieving nuclear fusion
To clarify why magnetic confinement techniques are necessary
